title: Common Features of Wave Behaviour — Hard

questions.question: A ripple tank experiment shows water waves crossing from a deep region to a shallow region. Which set of changes is correct?
options: Speed: Decreases; Wavelength: Decreases; Frequency: Unchanged
Speed: Increases; Wavelength: Increases; Frequency: Unchanged
Speed: Decreases; Wavelength: Unchanged; Frequency: Decreases
Speed: Unchanged; Wavelength: Decreases; Frequency: Increases
questions.explanation:
Shallow water provides more resistance, decreasing wave speed. Because the source frequency is constant, the wavelength must decrease to satisfy $v = f\lambda$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Radio signals have much longer wavelengths than TV signals. Why is it easier to receive radio signals behind a hill than TV signals?
options: Radio waves diffract more around the hill due to their longer wavelength
Radio waves reflect better off the atmosphere
TV signals travel faster and miss the receiver
Radio waves are longitudinal and travel through the hill
questions.explanation:
Waves with longer wavelengths relative to the size of an obstacle (the hill) undergo more diffraction, curving more effectively to reach the area behind the obstacle.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: In a refraction experiment, waves travel from region X to region Y. The angle of incidence is $35^circ$ and the angle of refraction is $20^circ$. What can be concluded about the regions?
options: Region Y is shallower than region X
Region Y is deeper than region X
The waves travel faster in region Y
The frequency is lower in region Y
questions.explanation:
Since the angle of refraction ($20^circ$) is smaller than the angle of incidence ($35^circ$), the wave has bent towards the normal. This indicates the wave slowed down, meaning region Y is shallower.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which combination of wavelength $\lambda$ and gap width $w$ will produce wavefronts that are nearly circular after passing through the gap?
options: $\lambda = 10\text{ cm}, w = 2\text{ cm}$
$\lambda = 2\text{ cm}, w = 10\text{ cm}$
$\lambda = 5\text{ cm}, w = 20\text{ cm}$
$\lambda = 1\text{ cm}, w = 15\text{ cm}$
questions.explanation:
Maximum diffraction (circular spreading) occurs when the wavelength is similar to or larger than the gap width. In the first option, the wavelength is much larger than the gap.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: When waves undergo reflection at a straight barrier, what happens to the frequency and the wavefront spacing?
options: Frequency: Unchanged; Spacing: Unchanged
Frequency: Increases; Spacing: Decreases
Frequency: Unchanged; Spacing: Decreases
Frequency: Decreases; Spacing: Unchanged
questions.explanation:
Reflection involves only a change in direction. The wave speed and frequency remain constant; therefore, the distance between wavefronts (wavelength) does not change.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.
